title: Von dem Machandelboom
source: Brüder Grimm, Kinder- und Hausmärchen, Band 1, 7. Auflage (Ausgabe letzter Hand), Dieterich, Göttingen 1857, KHM 47. Text from Wikisource (twice proofread against the original). 1857 spelling preserved exactly. This tale is in Low German (Plattdeutsch); the glossary gives the High German equivalent for each dialect form.
about: “Von dem Machandelboom” (The Juniper Tree, KHM 47) is one of the darkest and most celebrated of the Grimms’ tales, and it is told entirely in Low German (Plattdeutsch), the old speech of the north — not the High German of most of the collection. A wife wishes for a child as red as blood and white as snow, bears a son under the juniper tree and dies; the jealous stepmother beheads the boy with the lid of an apple-chest and cooks him into a black-pudding stew that his unknowing father eats, while his half-sister Marleenken buries the bones under the juniper, from which the boy rises as a bird whose song names the murder. Because the tale is in dialect, you will meet its regular sound-swaps against High German throughout: dat = das, de = der/die, wöör = war, un = und, Fru = Frau, sik = sich, ik = ich, wat = was, Vagel = Vogel, Köhm/köhm = kam, and the many ö- and ü-forms (äten = essen, äwer = über). The bird’s recurring song is given mostly in High German with a Low German refrain.

sentences.t: Dat is nu all lang heer, wol twe dusend Johr, do wöör dar en ryk Mann, de hadd ene schöne frame Fru, un se hadden sik beyde sehr leef, hadden awerst kene Kinner, se wünschden sik awerst sehr welke, un de Fru bedd’d so veel dorüm Dag un Nacht, man se kregen keen un kregen keen.
sentences.l: It is now all a long time ago, fully two thousand years, there was a rich man, who had a beautiful, pious wife, and they both loved each other very much, but had no children; they wished for some very much, and the wife prayed so much for it day and night, but they got none and got none.
sentences.i: It is now a long time ago, fully two thousand years, there was a rich man who had a beautiful, godly wife, and the two loved each other dearly, but they had no children; they longed for some, and the wife prayed for it day and night, but they got none, and none again.
sentences.n: This tale is told in Low German (Plattdeutsch), the old speech of northern Germany — expect it throughout. Note its regular sound-swaps against High German: dat = das (‘the/that/it’), de = der/die (‘the’), wöör = war (‘was’), un = und (‘and’), Fru = Frau, Kinner = Kinder, sik = sich, awerst = aber (‘but’), keen = kein (‘no, none’). “bedd’d” = betete (prayed); the clipped ’d is the Low German past ending. “man” here = ‘but, only.’

sentences.t: Vör erem Huse wöör en Hof, dorup stünn en Machandelboom, ünner dem stünn de Fru eens im Winter un schelld sik enen Appel, un as se sik den Appel so schelld, so sneet se sik in’n Finger un dat Blood feel in den Snee.
sentences.l: Before her house was a yard, thereon stood a juniper tree; under it the wife stood once in winter and peeled herself an apple, and as she so peeled the apple, she cut herself in the finger and the blood fell into the snow.
sentences.i: In front of her house was a yard, and on it stood a juniper tree; under it the wife stood one winter’s day peeling an apple, and as she peeled it she cut her finger, and the blood fell into the snow.
sentences.n: The Machandelboom (High German Machandelbaum / Wacholderbaum, the juniper) is the heart of the tale — the tree of wishing, death, burial and rebirth that binds it together. “schellen” = schälen (to peel); “Blood” = Blut; “Snee” = Schnee. The white snow and red blood set up the wish that follows.

sentences.t: „Ach,“ säd de Fru, un süft’d so recht hoog up, un seg dat Blood vör sik an, un wöör so recht wehmödig, „hadd ik doch en Kind, so rood as Blood un so witt as Snee.“
sentences.l: “Oh,” said the wife, and sighed right deeply, and looked at the blood before her, and grew right wistful, “had I but a child, as red as blood and as white as snow.”
sentences.i: “Oh,” said the wife, and sighed from the depths of her heart, and looked at the blood before her, and grew truly wistful: “If only I had a child, as red as blood and as white as snow.”
sentences.n: “säd” = sagte (said); “süft’d … up” = seufzte auf (sighed, separable auf-); “rood” = rot, “witt” = weiß. “hadd ik doch” is a wishing turn, ‘if only I had’ — the doch carries the longing. The red-and-white child is the wish that shapes the whole story.

sentences.t: Un as se dat säd, so wurr ehr so recht fröhlich to Mode: ehr wöör recht, as schull dat wat warden.
sentences.l: And as she said that, she grew so right merry in mood: it seemed to her just as if something should come of it.
sentences.i: And as she said it, her heart grew truly glad; she felt as though something would indeed come of it.
sentences.n: “wurr” = wurde (became); “ehr … to Mode” = ihr … zumute (how one feels), so ‘she felt cheerful.’ “as schull dat wat warden” = als sollte daraus etwas werden. “warden” = werden (to become) recurs in this dialect.

sentences.t: Do güng se to dem Huse, un’t güng een Maand hen, de Snee vorgüng:
sentences.l: Then she went into the house, and one month went by, the snow passed away:
sentences.n: The story now counts the pregnancy month by month, each with its sign in nature. “güng” = ging (went); “Maand” = Monat; “hen” = hin (away, by); “vorgüng” = verging (passed); “un’t” = und es.

sentences.t: un twe Maand, do wöör dat gröön:
sentences.l: and two months, then it was green:
sentences.i: and after two months, everything was green:
sentences.n: “twe” = zwei; “gröön” = grün (green).

sentences.t: un dre Maand, do kömen de Blömer uut der Eerd:
sentences.l: and three months, then the flowers came out of the earth:
sentences.n: “dre” = drei; “Blömer” = Blumen; “uut” = aus (out of); “Eerd” = Erde.

sentences.t: un veer Maand, do drungen sik alle Bömer in dat Holt, un de grönen Twyge wören all in eenanner wussen:
sentences.l: and four months, then all the trees pressed together in the wood, and the green twigs had all grown into one another:
sentences.i: and after four months, all the trees in the wood pressed close, and the green branches had grown into one another:
sentences.n: “veer” = vier; “Bömer” = Bäume; “Holt” = Holz, here ‘wood, forest’; “Twyge” = Zweige; “wören … wussen” = waren … gewachsen; “eenanner” = einander.

sentences.t: door süngen de Vögelkens dat dat ganße Holt schalld, un de Blöiten felen von den Bömern:
sentences.l: there the little birds sang so that the whole wood resounded, and the blossoms fell from the trees:
sentences.n: “süngen” = sangen; “Vögelkens” = Vögelchen; “ganße” = ganze; “schalld” = schallte (resounded); “Blöiten” = Blüten; “felen” = fielen.

sentences.t: do wöör de fofte Maand wech, un se stünn ünner dem Machandelboom, de röök so schön, do sprüng ehr dat Hart vör Freuden, un se füll up ere Knee un kunn sik nich laten:
sentences.l: then the fifth month was gone, and she stood under the juniper tree, which smelled so lovely; then her heart sprang for joy, and she fell upon her knees and could not contain herself:
sentences.i: then the fifth month was gone, and she stood under the juniper tree, which smelled so sweet, and her heart leapt for joy; she fell to her knees and could not contain herself:
sentences.n: “fofte” = fünfte; “wech” = weg (gone); “röök” = roch (smelled); “sprüng” = sprang; “Hart” = Herz; “füll” = fiel; “ere Knee” = ihre Knie; “kunn sik nich laten” = konnte sich nicht lassen (could not help herself).

sentences.t: un as de soste Maand vorby wöör, do wurren de Früchte dick un staark, do wurr se ganß still:
sentences.l: and when the sixth month was past, the fruits grew thick and strong, then she grew quite still:
sentences.n: “soste” = sechste; “vorby” = vorbei; “wurren” = wurden; “staark” = stark; “ganß” = ganz.

sentences.t: un de söwde Maand, do greep se na den Machandelbeeren un eet se so nydsch, do wurr se trurig un krank:
sentences.l: and the seventh month, then she reached for the juniper berries and ate them so greedily, then she grew sad and sick:
sentences.i: and in the seventh month she reached for the juniper berries and ate them so greedily that she grew sad and ill:
sentences.n: “söwde” = siebente; “greep” = griff; “Machandelbeeren” = Wacholderbeeren; “eet” = aß; “nydsch” = gierig/naschhaft (greedily); “trurig” = traurig.

sentences.t: do güng de achte Maand hen, un se reep eren Mann un weend un säd „wenn ik staarw, so begraaf my ünner den Machandelboom.“
sentences.l: then the eighth month went by, and she called her husband and wept and said, “when I die, then bury me under the juniper tree.”
sentences.n: “achte” = achte; “reep” = rief; “weend” = weinte; “staarw” = sterbe; “begraaf” = begrabe (bury!). Her dying wish ties her — and soon her son — to the juniper.

sentences.t: Do wurr se ganß getrost, un freude sik, bet de neegte Maand vorby wöör, do kreeg se en Kind so witt as Snee un so rood as Blood, un as se dat seeg, so freude se sik so, dat se stürw.
sentences.l: Then she grew quite comforted, and rejoiced, until the ninth month was past; then she got a child as white as snow and as red as blood, and when she saw it, she rejoiced so that she died.
sentences.i: Then she was quite comforted and glad, until the ninth month was over; then she bore a child as white as snow and as red as blood, and when she saw it she rejoiced so greatly that she died.
sentences.n: “bet” = bis (until); “neegte” = neunte; “kreeg” = kriegte/bekam (got, bore); “seeg” = sah; “stürw” = starb. The wish is granted and kills her — the red-and-white child of the opening is born.

sentences.t: Do begroof ehr Mann se ünner den Machandelboom, un he füng an to wenen so sehr; ene Tyd lang, do wurr dat wat sachter, un do he noch wat weend hadd, do hüll he up, un noch en Tyd, do nöhm he sik wedder ene Fru.
sentences.l: Then her husband buried her under the juniper tree, and he began to weep so sorely; for a while it grew somewhat gentler, and when he had wept a while more, he stopped, and after another time, he took himself a wife again.
sentences.i: Then her husband buried her under the juniper tree, and he began to weep bitterly; after a while his grief grew a little softer, and when he had wept a while longer, he ceased, and after a time more he took another wife.
sentences.n: “begroof” = begrub (buried); “füng an” = fing an (began, separable an-); “sachter” = sanfter/sachter; “hüll … up” = hielt auf (stopped); “nöhm” = nahm; “wedder” = wieder (again). The mother lies under the juniper; note how quickly the tale moves the father on to a second wife.

sentences.t: Mit de tweden Fru kreeg he ene Dochter, dat Kind awerst von der eersten Fru wöör en lüttje Sähn, un wöör so rood as Blood un so witt as Snee.
sentences.l: With the second wife he got a daughter, but the child of the first wife was a little son, and was as red as blood and as white as snow.
sentences.n: “tweden” = zweiten; “Dochter” = Tochter; “eersten” = ersten; “lüttje” = kleine (little) — a very common Low German word; “Sähn” = Sohn. The boy carries the dead mother’s red-and-white mark.

sentences.t: Wenn de Fru ere Dochter so anseeg, so hadd se se so leef, awerst denn seeg se den lüttjen Jung an, un dat güng ehr so dorch’t Hart, un ehr düchd as stünn he ehr allerwegen im Weg, un dachd denn man jümmer wo se ehr Dochter all das Vörmägent towenden wull, un de Böse gaf ehr dat in, dat se dem lüttjen Jung ganß gramm wurr un stödd em herüm von een Eck in de anner, un buffd em hier un knuffd em door, so dat dat aarme Kind jümmer in Angst wöör.
sentences.l: When the wife looked at her daughter, she loved her so; but then she looked at the little boy, and it went to her so through the heart, and it seemed to her as if he stood in her way everywhere, and she thought then only always how she might turn all the property over to her daughter, and the Evil One put it into her, so that she grew quite spiteful toward the little boy and shoved him about from one corner into the other, and cuffed him here and knocked him there, so that the poor child was always in fear.
sentences.i: When the wife looked at her own daughter, she loved her dearly; but then she would look at the little boy, and it cut her to the heart, and it seemed to her as though he stood in her way at every turn, and she thought of nothing but how she might turn the whole inheritance over to her daughter; and the Evil One put it into her heart, so that she grew quite spiteful toward the little boy, shoving him about from one corner to another, cuffing him here and knocking him there, until the poor child lived in constant fear.
sentences.n: “anseeg / seeg … an” = ansah (separable); “dorch’t Hart” = durch das Herz; “düchd” = däuchte/dünkte (seemed); “allerwegen” = überall; “man jümmer” = nur immer; “Vörmägent” = Vermögen (property, inheritance); “towenden” = zuwenden (turn over to); “de Böse” = der Böse, the Evil One (the Devil); “gramm” = gram (spiteful); “stödd” = stieß; “buffd/knuffd” = puffte/knuffte. The stepmother’s cruelty takes shape.

sentences.t: Wenn he denn uut de School köhm, so hadd he kene ruhige Städ.
sentences.l: When he then came out of school, he had no quiet place.
sentences.i: So whenever he came home from school, he had no peace anywhere.
sentences.n: “köhm” = kam; “ruhige Städ” = ruhige Stätte/Stelle (a quiet spot); “kene” = keine.

sentences.t: Eens wöör de Fru up de Kamer gaan, do köhm de lüttje Dochter ook herup un säd „Moder, gif my enen Appel.“
sentences.l: Once the wife had gone up to the chamber, then the little daughter came up too and said, “Mother, give me an apple.”
sentences.n: “Eens” = einmal; “up de Kamer gaan” = auf die Kammer gegangen; “herup” = herauf; “Moder” = Mutter; “gif” = gib (give!).

sentences.t: „Ja, myn Kind“ säd de Fru un gaf ehr enen schönen Appel uut der Kist; de Kist awerst hadd enen grooten sworen Deckel mit en groot schaarp ysern Slott.
sentences.l: “Yes, my child,” said the wife and gave her a fine apple out of the chest; but the chest had a great heavy lid with a big sharp iron lock.
sentences.n: “myn” = mein; “Kist” = Kiste; “sworen” = schweren; “schaarp” = scharf; “ysern” = eisern; “Slott” = Schloss (lock). The lidded chest is the murder weapon to come — note it.

sentences.t: „Moder,“ säd de lüttje Dochter, „schall Broder nich ook enen hebben?“
sentences.l: “Mother,” said the little daughter, “shall brother not have one too?”
sentences.n: “schall” = soll (shall); “Broder” = Bruder; “hebben” = haben. Marleenken’s kindness to her brother runs right through the tale.

sentences.t: Dat vördrööt de Fru, doch säd se „ja, wenn he uut de School kummt.“
sentences.l: That vexed the wife, yet she said, “yes, when he comes out of school.”
sentences.n: “vördrööt” = verdross (vexed, verdrießen); “doch” = doch; “kummt” = kommt.

sentences.t: Un as se uut dat Fenster wohr wurr dat he köhm, so wöör dat recht, as wenn de Böse äwer ehr köhm, un se grappst to un nöhm erer Dochter den Appel wedder wech un säd „du schalst nich ehr enen hebben as Broder.“
sentences.l: And when she became aware out of the window that he was coming, it was just as if the Evil One came over her, and she grabbed and took the apple away from her daughter again and said, “you shall not have one before brother.”
sentences.i: And when she saw through the window that he was coming, it was as though the Evil One came over her, and she snatched the apple back from her daughter and said, “You shall not have one before your brother.”
sentences.n: “wohr wurr” = gewahr wurde (became aware); “äwer ehr köhm” = über sie käme; “grappst to” = grapschte zu (grabbed); “wech” = weg; “schalst” = sollst; “ehr … as” = eher … als (sooner … than).

sentences.t: Do smeet se den Appel in de Kist un maakd de Kist to: do köhm de lüttje Jung in de Döhr, do gaf ehr de Böse in dat se fründlich to em säd „myn Sähn, wullt du enen Appel hebben?“ un seeg em so hastig an.
sentences.l: Then she threw the apple into the chest and shut the chest to; then the little boy came in the door, then the Evil One put it into her that she said kindly to him, “my son, do you want an apple?” and looked at him so keenly.
sentences.i: Then she threw the apple into the chest and closed it; and the little boy came in at the door, and the Evil One put it into her, so that she said kindly to him, “My son, will you have an apple?” — and looked at him with a fierce eye.
sentences.n: “smeet” = schmiss/warf; “maakd … to” = machte zu (shut, separable zu-); “Döhr” = Tür; “fründlich” = freundlich; “wullt” = willst; “hastig” = hastig. Her false kindness sets the trap.

sentences.t: „Moder,“ säd de lüttje Jung, „wat sühst du gräsig uut! ja, gif my enen Appel.“
sentences.l: “Mother,” said the little boy, “how ghastly you look! yes, give me an apple.”
sentences.n: “wat … uut” = wie … aus (aussehen, to look); “gräsig” = gräßlich (ghastly). The child sees something wrong in her face.

sentences.t: Do wöör ehr as schull se em toreden.
sentences.l: Then it seemed to her as if she must coax him.
sentences.i: Then she felt she must urge him on.
sentences.n: “toreden” = zureden (to coax, urge; separable zu-). “as schull se” = als sollte sie.

sentences.t: „Kumm mit my,“ säd se un maakd den Deckel up, „hahl dy enen Appel heruut.“
sentences.l: “Come with me,” she said and opened the lid, “fetch yourself an apple out.”
sentences.n: “Kumm mit my” = komm mit mir; “maakd … up” = machte auf; “hahl” = hol; “dy” = dir; “heruut” = heraus.

sentences.t: Un as sik de lüttje Jung henin bückd, so reet ehr de Böse, bratsch! slöög se den Deckel to dat de Kopp afflöög un ünner de roden Appel füll.
sentences.l: And as the little boy bent himself in, the Evil One drove her — crash! — she struck the lid to, so that the head flew off and fell among the red apples.
sentences.i: And as the little boy leaned in, the Evil One seized her — crash! — she slammed the lid down, so that his head flew off and fell among the red apples.
sentences.n: The murder, told plainly, as the tale intends. “henin bückd” = hinein bückte; “reet” = riss (drove, seized); “bratsch” is the crash of the lid; “slöög … to” = schlug zu (slammed shut); “Kopp” = Kopf; “afflöög” = abflog; “füll” = fiel.

sentences.t: Da äwerleep ehr dat in de Angst, un dachd „kunn ik dat von my bringen!“
sentences.l: Then it ran over her in her fear, and she thought, “could I but get that off myself!”
sentences.i: Then a wave of dread ran over her, and she thought, “If only I could put this off on someone else!”
sentences.n: “äwerleep” = überlief (ran over); “dachd” = dachte; “von my bringen” = von mir bringen (‘get it away from me,’ shift the blame).

sentences.t: Da güng se bawen na ere Stuw na erem Draagkasten un hahl’ uut de bäwelste Schuuflad enen witten Dook, un sett’t den Kopp wedder up den Hals un bünd den Halsdook so üm, dat’n niks sehn kunn, un sett’t em vör de Döhr up enen Stohl un gaf em den Appel in de Hand.
sentences.l: Then she went up to her room to her chest of drawers and fetched out of the topmost drawer a white cloth, and set the head back on the neck and bound the neckerchief around so that one could see nothing, and set him before the door on a chair and gave him the apple in his hand.
sentences.i: Then she went up to her room, to her dresser, and took a white cloth from the top drawer, set the head back on the neck and tied the kerchief round it so that nothing could be seen, and sat him on a chair before the door with the apple in his hand.
sentences.n: “bawen” = oben; “Stuw” = Stube; “Draagkasten” = Tragkasten/Kommode; “bäwelste Schuuflad” = oberste Schublade; “witten Dook” = weißen Tuch; “sett’t” = setzte; “bünd” = band; “Halsdook” = Halstuch; “dat’n” = dass man; “niks” = nichts; “Stohl” = Stuhl. She stages the dead boy to look alive — setting up Marleenken’s blow.

sentences.t: Do köhm doorna Marleenken to erer Moder in de Kääk de stünn by dem Führ un hadd enen Putt mit heet Water vör sik, den röhrd se jümmer üm.
sentences.l: Then afterward Marleenken came to her mother in the kitchen, who stood by the fire and had a pot with hot water before her, which she kept stirring round.
sentences.i: Then Marleenken came to her mother in the kitchen, who was standing by the fire with a pot of hot water before her, which she kept stirring.
sentences.n: “doorna” = danach; “Kääk” = Küche; “Führ” = Feuer; “Putt” = Pott/Topf; “heet Water” = heißes Wasser; “röhrd … üm” = rührte um (separable um-); “jümmer” = immer (kept).

sentences.t: „Moder,“ säd Marleenken, „Broder sitt vör de Döhr un süht ganß witt uut un hett enen Appel in de Hand, ik heb em beden he schull my den Appel gewen, awerst he antwöörd my nich, do wurr my ganß grolich.“
sentences.l: “Mother,” said Marleenken, “brother sits before the door and looks quite white and has an apple in his hand; I asked him to give me the apple, but he does not answer me, and I grew quite frightened.”
sentences.i: “Mother,” said Marleenken, “brother is sitting by the door and looks quite pale and has an apple in his hand; I asked him to give me the apple, but he won’t answer me, and it made me feel quite dreadful.”
sentences.n: “sitt” = sitzt; “süht … uut” = sieht aus; “hett” = hat; “beden” = gebeten (asked); “gewen” = geben; “antwöörd” = antwortete; “grolich” = grulig/gräulich (eerie). Marleenken senses the horror without understanding it.

sentences.t: „Gah nochmaal hen,“ säd de Moder, „un wenn he dy nich antworden will, so gif em eens an de Oren.“
sentences.l: “Go there once more,” said the mother, “and if he will not answer you, then give him one on the ears.”
sentences.n: “Gah … hen” = geh hin; “nochmaal” = nochmal; “antworden” = antworten; “gif em eens an de Oren” = gib ihm eins an die Ohren (box his ears). The mother sends the innocent girl to strike the corpse.

sentences.t: Do güng Marleenken hen un säd, „Broder, gif my den Appel.“
sentences.l: Then Marleenken went there and said, “brother, give me the apple.”

sentences.t: Awerst he sweeg still, do gaf se em eens up de Oren, do feel de Kopp herünn, doräwer vörschrock se sik un füng an to wenen un to roren, un löp to erer Moder un säd „ach, Moder, ik hebb mynem Broder den Kopp afslagen,“ un weend un weend un wull sik nich tofreden gewen.
sentences.l: But he stayed silent, so she gave him one on the ears, then the head fell down; at that she took fright and began to weep and to bawl, and ran to her mother and said, “oh, mother, I have struck my brother’s head off,” and wept and wept and would not be comforted.
sentences.i: But he stayed silent, so she boxed his ears, and the head fell off; at that she was terrified and began to weep and wail, and ran to her mother and said, “Oh, mother, I’ve knocked my brother’s head off!” and wept and wept and would not be comforted.
sentences.n: “sweeg still” = schwieg still; “feel … herünn” = fiel herunter; “vörschrock” = erschrak (took fright); “roren” = plärren/röhren (to bawl); “löp” = lief; “afslagen” = abgeschlagen; “wull sik nich tofreden gewen” = wollte sich nicht zufrieden geben (would not be consoled). The guilt is laid on the child, though the stepmother did the deed.

sentences.t: „Marleenken,“ säd de Moder, „wat hest du dahn! awerst swyg man still, dat et keen Mensch maarkt, dat is nu doch nich to ännern; wy willen em in Suhr kaken.“
sentences.l: “Marleenken,” said the mother, “what have you done! but be silent, so that no one notices, it cannot be helped now; we will cook him in sour stew.”
sentences.i: “Marleenken,” said the mother, “what have you done! But hush now, so that no one finds out — it can’t be undone; we’ll cook him into black-pudding stew.”
sentences.n: “hest du dahn” = hast du getan; “swyg man still” = schweig nur still; “maarkt” = merkt; “to ännern” = zu ändern; “wy” = wir; “Suhr” = Sauer, a sour stew of blood and meat (the Swartsuhr below). The stepmother’s cold cover-up: she will cook the boy and feed him to the father.

sentences.t: Do nöhm de Moder den lüttjen Jung un hackd em in Stücken, ded de in den Putt un kaakd em in Suhr.
sentences.l: Then the mother took the little boy and hacked him into pieces, put them in the pot and cooked him in sour stew.
sentences.n: “hackd” = hackte; “Stücken” = Stücke; “ded” = tat (put); “kaakd” = kochte. The grim deed is stated flatly, as the story does throughout.

sentences.t: Marleenken awerst stünn daarby un weend un weend, un de Tranen füllen all in den Putt un se bruukden goor keen Solt.
sentences.l: But Marleenken stood by and wept and wept, and the tears all fell into the pot, and they needed no salt at all.
sentences.n: “daarby” = dabei; “Tranen” = Tränen; “füllen” = fielen; “bruukden” = brauchten; “goor keen Solt” = gar kein Salz. Marleenken’s tears salt the dreadful dish.

sentences.t: Do köhm de Vader to Huus un sett’t sik to Disch un säd „wo is denn myn Sähn?“
sentences.l: Then the father came home and sat himself at the table and said, “where then is my son?”
sentences.n: “Vader” = Vater; “sett’t sik” = setzte sich; “Disch” = Tisch; “wo” = wo; “denn” = denn. The father’s repeated question drives the scene.

sentences.t: Da droog de Moder ene groote groote Schöttel up mit Swartsuhr, un Marleenken weend un kunn sich nich hollen.
sentences.l: Then the mother brought up a great, great dish with black-pudding stew, and Marleenken wept and could not contain herself.
sentences.n: “droog … up” = trug auf (served); “Schöttel” = Schüssel; “Swartsuhr” = Schwarzsauer, a dark stew made with blood; “hollen” = halten (contain). The father is about to eat his own son.

sentences.t: Do säd de Vader wedder „wo is denn myn Sähn?“
sentences.l: Then the father said again, “where then is my son?”

sentences.t: „Ach,“ säd de Moder, „he is äwer Land gaan, na Mütten erer Grootöhm: he wull door wat blywen.“
sentences.l: “Oh,” said the mother, “he has gone across the land, to mother’s great-uncle: he wanted to stay there a while.”
sentences.i: “Oh,” said the mother, “he has gone off into the country, to his mother’s great-uncle; he wanted to stay there a while.”
sentences.n: “äwer Land gaan” = über Land gegangen; “Mütten” = der Mutter (mother’s); “Grootöhm” = Großoheim/Großonkel (great-uncle); “blywen” = bleiben. The stepmother’s lie.

sentences.t: „Wat dait he denn door? un heft my nich maal Adjüüs sechd!“
sentences.l: “What is he doing there then? and hasn’t even said goodbye to me!”
sentences.n: “Wat dait he” = was tut er; “door” = da/dort; “heft” = hat; “nich maal” = nicht mal; “Adjüüs” = ade/adieu (goodbye), Low German from French adieu; “sechd” = gesagt.

sentences.t: „O he wull geern hen un bed my of he door wol sos Wäken blywen kunn; he is jo woll door uphawen.“
sentences.l: “Oh, he wanted gladly to go, and asked me whether he might stay there fully six weeks; he is surely well looked after there.”
sentences.i: “Oh, he was eager to go, and asked me if he might stay there a good six weeks; he’s well taken care of there.”
sentences.n: “geern” = gern; “bed” = bat (asked, bitten); “of” = ob; “sos Wäken” = sechs Wochen; “uphawen” = aufgehoben (looked after).

sentences.t: „Ach,“ säd de Mann, „my is so recht trurig dat is doch nich recht, he hadd my doch Adjüüs sagen schullt.“
sentences.l: “Oh,” said the man, “I feel so truly sad; it is not right, he ought to have said goodbye to me.”
sentences.i: “Oh,” said the man, “it makes me so sad — it isn’t right; he ought to have said goodbye to me.”
sentences.n: “my is … trurig” = mir ist … traurig (dative of feeling); “schullt” = gesollt/sollen (ought to have). Even now the father grieves, not knowing he is eating the boy.

sentences.t: Mit des füng he an to äten un säd „Marleenken, wat weenst du? Broder wart wol wedder kamen.“
sentences.l: With that he began to eat and said, “Marleenken, why do you weep? Brother will surely come again.”
sentences.n: “Mit des” = mit dem/damit; “äten” = essen; “weenst” = weinst; “wart” = wird; “wol” = wohl (surely); “kamen” = kommen.

sentences.t: „Ach, Fru,“ säd he do, „wat smeckt my dat Äten schöön? gif my mehr!“
sentences.l: “Oh, wife,” he said then, “how good this food tastes to me? give me more!”
sentences.i: “Oh, wife,” he said, “how good this food tastes! Give me more!”
sentences.n: “Fru” = Frau; “smeckt my … schöön” = schmeckt mir … schön (dative); “gif” = gib; “mehr” = mehr. The horror of the father relishing the dish.

sentences.t: Un je mehr he eet, je mehr wull he hebben, un säd „geeft my mehr, gy schöhlt niks door af hebben, dat is as wenn dat all myn wör.“
sentences.l: And the more he ate, the more he wanted to have, and said, “give me more, you shall have none of it, it is as if it were all mine.”
sentences.i: And the more he ate, the more he wanted, and said, “Give me more — you two shall have none of it; it’s as though it were all meant for me alone.”
sentences.n: “je … je” = je … je (the more … the more); “geeft” = gebt; “gy” = ihr; “schöhlt” = sollt; “niks … af hebben” = nichts davon haben; “wör” = wäre. He unknowingly claims his son entirely for himself.

sentences.t: Un he eet un eet, un de Knakens smeet he all ünner den Disch, bet he allens up hadd.
sentences.l: And he ate and ate, and threw all the bones under the table, until he had everything up.
sentences.i: And he ate and ate, and threw all the bones under the table, until he had finished it all.
sentences.n: “Knakens” = Knochen; “smeet” = schmiss/warf; “allens up hadd” = alles aufhatte. The bones under the table become the boy’s means of rebirth.

sentences.t: Marleenken awerst güng hen na ere Commod un nöhm ut de ünnerste Schuuf eren besten syden Dook, un hahl all de Beenkens un Knakens ünner den Disch heruut un bünd se in den syden Dook un droog se vör de Döhr un weend ere blödigen Tranen.
sentences.l: But Marleenken went to her chest of drawers and took from the bottom drawer her best silk cloth, and fetched all the little bones and bones out from under the table and bound them in the silk cloth and carried them before the door and wept her bloody tears.
sentences.i: But Marleenken went to her dresser and took her best silk kerchief from the bottom drawer, gathered all the little bones out from under the table, tied them in the silk cloth, carried them out before the door, and wept bitter, bloody tears.
sentences.n: “Commod” = Kommode; “ünnerste Schuuf” = unterste Schublade; “syden Dook” = seidenen Tuch; “Beenkens” = Beinchen (little bones); “blödigen” = blutigen. This gathering of the bones in a silk cloth is exactly what the bird will later sing.

sentences.t: Door läd se se ünner den Machandelboom in dat gröne Gras, un as se se door henlechd hadd’, so war ehr mit eenmal so recht licht, un weend nich mer.
sentences.l: There she laid them under the juniper tree in the green grass, and when she had laid them there, all at once she felt so light, and wept no more.
sentences.n: “läd” = legte; “henlechd” = hingelegt; “mit eenmal” = mit einem Mal; “licht” = leicht; “mer” = mehr. Burial under the juniper — the same tree as the mother — begins the rebirth.

sentences.t: Do füng de Machandelboom an sik to bewegen, un de Twyge deden sik jümmer so recht von eenanner, un denn wedder tohoop, so recht as wenn sik eener so recht freut un mit de Händ so dait.
sentences.l: Then the juniper tree began to move, and the twigs kept parting from one another, and then together again, just as when someone rejoices and does so with his hands.
sentences.i: Then the juniper tree began to stir, and its branches kept opening apart and then closing together again, just as when someone claps his hands for joy.
sentences.n: “füng an” = fing an; “bewegen” = bewegen; “Twyge” = Zweige; “deden sik … von eenanner” = taten sich auseinander; “tohoop” = zuhauf/zusammen (together); “freut” = freut; “Händ” = Hände; “dait” = tut.

sentences.t: Mit des so güng dar so’n Newel von dem Boom un recht in dem Newel dar brennd dat as Führ, un uut dem Führ dar flöög so’n schönen Vagel heruut, de süng so herrlich und flöög hoog in de Luft, un as he wech wöör, do wöör de Machandelboom as he vörhen west wöör, un de Dook mit de Knakens wöör wech.
sentences.l: With that there went such a mist from the tree, and right in the mist it burned like fire, and out of the fire there flew such a beautiful bird, which sang so gloriously and flew high in the air; and when it was gone, the juniper tree was as it had been before, and the cloth with the bones was gone.
sentences.i: With that a mist rose from the tree, and in the very heart of the mist it burned like fire, and out of the fire flew a beautiful bird that sang gloriously and flew high into the air; and when it was gone, the juniper tree was just as before, and the cloth with the bones had vanished.
sentences.n: “so’n” = so ein; “Newel” = Nebel (mist); “Boom” = Baum; “brennd” = brannte; “Führ” = Feuer; “flöög” = flog; “Vagel” = Vogel; “süng” = sang; “herrlich” = herrlich; “hoog” = hoch; “wech” = weg; “vörhen west” = vorhin gewesen. The murdered boy is reborn as the bird — the tale’s turning point.

sentences.t: Marleenken awerst wöör so recht licht un vörgnöögt, recht as wenn de Broder noch leewd.
sentences.l: But Marleenken was so truly light and cheerful, just as if brother still lived.
sentences.n: “vörgnöögt” = vergnügt (cheerful); “leewd” = lebte. She senses her brother is not truly lost.

sentences.t: Do güng se wedder ganß lustig in dat Huus by Disch un eet.
sentences.l: Then she went again quite merrily into the house to the table and ate.
sentences.n: “lustig” = lustig; “by Disch” = bei Tisch. She, unlike the father, ate nothing of the stew — she gathered the bones instead.

sentences.t: De Vagel awerst flöög wech un sett’t sik up enen Goldsmidt syn Huus un füng an to singen
sentences.l: But the bird flew away and settled on a goldsmith’s house and began to sing
sentences.n: “Goldsmidt syn Huus” = Goldschmieds Haus — Low German marks possession with a name + syn (‘his’): ‘the goldsmith his house,’ i.e. the goldsmith’s house. “singen” = singen.

sentences.t:
„mein Mutter der mich schlacht,
mein Vater der mich aß,
mein Schwester der Marlenichen
sucht alle meine Benichen,
bind’t sie in ein seiden Tuch,
legt’s unter den Machandelbaum.
Kywitt, kywitt, wat vör’n schöön Vagel bün ik!“
sentences.l:
“my mother, she who slaughters me,
my father, he who ate me,
my sister, little Marlene,
seeks out all my little bones,
binds them in a silken cloth,
lays them under the juniper tree.
Peewit, peewit, what a handsome bird am I!”
sentences.i:
“My mother, she who slaughtered me,
my father, he who ate me,
my sister, little Marleen,
seeks out all my little bones,
binds them in a silken cloth,
and lays them under the juniper tree.
Kywitt, kywitt — what a handsome bird am I!”
sentences.n: The bird’s song — the heart of the tale — names the murder outright: the mother killed him, the father ate him, the sister gathered his bones and laid them under the juniper. It recurs many times, each with small variations (schlacht/schlachtet, bind’t/bindet, wat/was). “Benichen” and “Marlenichen” are tender song-diminutives (Beinchen ‘little bones,’ and the name Marleenken). “Kywitt” imitates the cry of the lapwing (Kiebitz). The song is here mostly High German, with the last line Low German: wat vör’n schöön Vagel bün ik = was für ein schöner Vogel bin ich (‘what a handsome bird am I’).

sentences.t: De Goldsmidt seet in syn Waarkstäd un maakd ene gollne Kede, do höörd he den Vagel, de up syn Dack seet un süng, un dat dünkd em so schöön.
sentences.l: The goldsmith sat in his workshop and made a golden chain; then he heard the bird that sat on his roof and sang, and it seemed to him so lovely.
sentences.n: “seet” = saß; “Waarkstäd” = Werkstatt; “gollne Kede” = goldene Kette; “höörd” = hörte; “Dack” = Dach; “dünkd” = dünkte (seemed). The bird trades its song for gifts — first the gold chain.

sentences.t: Da stünn he up, un as he äwer den Süll güng, do vörlöör he eenen Tüffel.
sentences.l: Then he stood up, and as he went over the threshold, he lost a slipper.
sentences.n: “stünn … up” = stand auf; “äwer den Süll” = über die Schwelle (over the threshold); “vörlöör” = verlor; “Tüffel” = Pantoffel (slipper).

sentences.t: He güng awer so recht midden up de Strat hen, eenen Tüffel un een Sock an: syn Schortfell hadd he vör, un in de een Hand hadd he de golln Kede un in de anner de Tang; un de Sünn schynd so hell up de Strat.
sentences.l: But he went right into the middle of the street, one slipper and one sock on: his apron he had on, and in the one hand he had the golden chain and in the other the tongs; and the sun shone so bright on the street.
sentences.i: But he walked right out into the middle of the street, one slipper on and one foot in its sock, his apron still on, the golden chain in one hand and the tongs in the other; and the sun shone bright on the street.
sentences.n: “midden up de Strat” = mitten auf die Straße; “Sock” = Socke; “Schortfell” = Schurzfell (leather apron); “Tang” = Zange (tongs); “Sünn schynd” = Sonne schien; “hell” = hell (bright).

sentences.t: Door güng he recht so staan un seeg den Vagel an.
sentences.l: There he came to a stand just so and looked at the bird.
sentences.i: There he stopped just as he was and looked at the bird.
sentences.n: “güng … staan” = ging/blieb stehen (came to a stop); “seeg … an” = sah an.

sentences.t: „Vagel,“ secht he do, „wo schöön kanst du singen! Sing my dat Stück nochmaal.“
sentences.l: “Bird,” says he then, “how beautifully you can sing! Sing me that piece once more.”
sentences.n: “secht” = sagt; “wo schöön” = wie schön; “kanst” = kannst; “dat Stück” = das Stück; “nochmaal” = nochmal.

sentences.t: „Ne,“ secht de Vagel, „twemaal sing ik nich umsünst. Gif my de golln Kede, so will ik dy’t nochmaal singen.“
sentences.l: “No,” says the bird, “twice I do not sing for nothing. Give me the golden chain, then I will sing it to you again.”
sentences.n: “Ne” = nein; “twemaal” = zweimal; “umsünst” = umsonst (for nothing); “golln” = goldene; “dy’t” = dir es. The bird’s refrain-bargain, repeated with each craftsman.

sentences.t: „Door,“ secht de Goldsmidt, „hest du de golln Kede, nu sing my dat nochmaal.“
sentences.l: “There,” says the goldsmith, “you have the golden chain, now sing me that again.”
sentences.n: “Door” = da (here you are); “hest” = hast.

sentences.t: Do köhm de Vagel un nöhm de golln Kede so in de rechte Poot, un güng vor den Goldsmidt sitten un süng
sentences.l: Then the bird came and took the golden chain in its right claw, and went to sit before the goldsmith and sang
sentences.n: “nöhm” = nahm; “Poot” = Pfote (paw/claw); “vor … sitten” = vor … sitzen; “süng” = sang.

sentences.t:
„mein Mutter der mich schlacht,
mein Vater der mich aß,
mein Schwester der Marlenichen
sucht alle meine Benichen,
sentences.l:
“my mother, she who slaughters me,
my father, he who ate me,
my sister, little Marlene,
seeks out all my little bones,
sentences.n: The song again, now given for the gold chain (printed here in two parts).

sentences.t:
bindt sie in ein seiden Tuch,
legts unter den Machandelbaum.
Kywitt, kywitt, was vör’n schöön Vagel bün ik!“
sentences.l:
binds them in a silken cloth,
lays them under the juniper tree.
Peewit, peewit, what a handsome bird am I!”
sentences.n: Note the small variations from the first singing: “bindt” for “bind’t,” “was” for “wat” — tiny shifts recur each time the song returns; they are in the original and kept as printed.

sentences.t: Do flög de Vagel wech na enem Schooster un sett’t sik up den syn Dack un süng
sentences.l: Then the bird flew away to a shoemaker and settled on his roof and sang
sentences.n: “flög” = flog; “Schooster” = Schuster (shoemaker); “den syn Dack” = dessen Dach — again the Low German possessive with syn: ‘the (shoemaker) his roof.’

sentences.t:
„mein Mutter der mich schlacht,
mein Vater der mich aß,
mein Schwester der Marlenichen
sucht alle meine Benichen,
bindet sie in ein seiden Tuch,
legts unter den Machandelbaum.
Kywitt, kywitt, wat vör’n schöön Vagel bün ik!“
sentences.l:
“my mother, she who slaughters me,
my father, he who ate me,
my sister, little Marlene,
seeks out all my little bones,
binds them in a silken cloth,
lays them under the juniper tree.
Peewit, peewit, what a handsome bird am I!”
sentences.n: The full song once more, for the shoemaker; “bindet” is this time’s small variant of the ‘binds’ line.

sentences.t: De Schooster höörd dat un leep vör syn Döhr in Hemdsaarmels, un seeg na syn Dack un mussd de Hand vör de Ogen hollen, dat de Sünn em nich blend’t.
sentences.l: The shoemaker heard it and ran before his door in shirtsleeves, and looked up at his roof and had to hold his hand before his eyes, so that the sun should not blind him.
sentences.n: “leep” = lief; “Hemdsaarmels” = Hemdsärmeln (shirtsleeves); “seeg na” = sah nach; “mussd” = musste; “Ogen” = Augen; “blend’t” = blendete (blinded).

sentences.t: „Vagel,“ secht he, „wat kannst du schöön singen.“
sentences.l: “Bird,” says he, “how beautifully you can sing.”
sentences.n: “wat … schöön” = wie schön.

sentences.t: Do rööp he in syn Döhr henin „Fru, kumm mal heruut, dar is een Vagel: süh mal den Vagel, de kann maal schöön singen.“
sentences.l: Then he called into his door, “wife, come out a moment, there is a bird: just look at the bird, it can sing so beautifully.”
sentences.n: “rööp” = rief; “henin” = hinein; “kumm mal heruut” = komm mal heraus; “süh mal” = sieh mal (just look); “maal” = mal (just).

sentences.t: Do rööp he syn Dochter un Kinner un Gesellen, Jung un Maagd, un se kömen all up de Strat un seegen den Vagel an wo schöön he wöör, un he hadd so recht rode un gröne Feddern, un üm den Hals wöör dat as luter Gold, un de Ogen blünken em im Koop as Steern.
sentences.l: Then he called his daughter and children and journeymen, lad and maid, and they all came out onto the street and looked at the bird, how beautiful it was, and it had such red and green feathers, and around its neck it was like pure gold, and its eyes glittered in its head like stars.
sentences.i: Then he called his daughter and children and journeymen, the lad and the maid, and they all came out into the street and gazed at the bird, how lovely it was; it had bright red and green feathers, and about its neck it seemed pure gold, and its eyes glittered in its head like stars.
sentences.n: “Kinner” = Kinder; “kömen” = kamen; “seegen … an” = sahen an; “Feddern” = Federn; “luter” = lauter (pure); “blünken” = blinkten; “Koop” = Kopf (variant of Kopp); “Steern” = Sterne.

sentences.t: „Vagel,“ sägd de Schooster, „nu sing my dat Stück nochmaal.“
sentences.l: “Bird,” said the shoemaker, “now sing me that piece again.”
sentences.n: “sägd” = sagte.

sentences.t: „Ne,“ secht de Vagel, „twemaal sing ik nich umsünst, du must my wat schenken.“
sentences.l: “No,” says the bird, “twice I do not sing for nothing, you must give me something.”
sentences.n: “must” = musst; “wat schenken” = etwas schenken.

sentences.t: „Fru,“ säd de Mann, „gah na dem Bähn: up dem bäwelsten Boord door staan een Poor rode Schö, de bring herünn.“
sentences.l: “Wife,” said the man, “go up to the loft: on the topmost board there stands a pair of red shoes, bring them down.”
sentences.n: “Bähn” = Boden/Dachboden (loft); “bäwelsten Boord” = obersten Bord; “Poor rode Schö” = Paar rote Schuhe; “bring herünn” = bring herunter.

sentences.t: Do güng de Fru hen un hahl de Schö.
sentences.l: Then the wife went and fetched the shoes.
sentences.n: “hahl” = holte; “Schö” = Schuhe.

sentences.t: „Door, Vagel,“ säd de Mann, „nu sing my dat Stück nochmaal.“
sentences.l: “There, bird,” said the man, “now sing me that piece again.”

sentences.t: Do köhm de Vagel un nöhm de Schö in de linke Klau, un flöög wedder up dat Dack un süng
sentences.l: Then the bird came and took the shoes in its left claw, and flew back up onto the roof and sang
sentences.n: “Klau” = Klaue (claw); “linke” = linke (left).

sentences.t:
„mein Mutter der mich schlacht,
mein Vater der mich aß,
sentences.l:
“my mother, she who slaughters me,
my father, he who ate me,
sentences.n: The song once more, now for the red shoes (split across two verse blocks).

sentences.t:
mein Schwester der Marlenichen
sucht alle meine Benichen
bindet sie in ein seiden Tuch,
legts unter den Machandelbaum.
Kywitt, kywitt, wat vör’n schöön Vagel bün ik!“
sentences.l:
my sister, little Marlene,
seeks out all my little bones,
binds them in a silken cloth,
lays them under the juniper tree.
Peewit, peewit, what a handsome bird am I!”

sentences.t: Un as he uutsungen hadd, so flöög he wech: de Kede hadd he in de rechte un de Schö in de linke Klau, un he flöög wyt wech na ene Mähl, un de Mähl güng „klippe klappe, klippe klappe, klippe klappe.“
sentences.l: And when he had sung out, he flew away: the chain he had in the right and the shoes in the left claw, and he flew far away to a mill, and the mill went ‘clip clap, clip clap, clip clap.’
sentences.n: “uutsungen” = ausgesungen (finished singing); “wyt wech” = weit weg; “Mähl” = Mühle (mill); “klippe klappe” imitates the clatter of the mill-wheel.

sentences.t: Un in de Mähl door seeten twintig Mählenburßen, de hauden enen Steen un hackden „hick hack, hick hack, hick hack,“ un de Mähl güng „klippe klappe, klippe klappe, klippe klappe.“
sentences.l: And in the mill there sat twenty mill-lads, who were hewing at a stone and hacking, ‘hick hack, hick hack, hick hack,’ and the mill went ‘clip clap, clip clap, clip clap.’
sentences.n: “seeten” = saßen; “twintig” = zwanzig; “Mählenburßen” = Mühlenburschen (mill-lads); “hauden” = hieben/hauten (hewed); “Steen” = Stein; “hackden” = hackten. The twenty lads dressing the millstone set up the last, heaviest gift.

sentences.t: Do güng de Vagel up enen Lindenboom sitten, de vör de Mähl stünn und süng
sentences.l: Then the bird went to sit on a linden tree that stood before the mill, and sang
sentences.n: “Lindenboom” = Lindenbaum (linden tree).

sentences.t: „mein Mutter der mich schlacht,“
sentences.l: “my mother, she who slaughters me,”
sentences.n: Now the song is broken up line by line — as the bird sings each line, more of the mill-lads stop their work to listen.

sentences.t: do höörd een up,
sentences.l: then one stopped,
sentences.n: “höörd … up” = hörte auf (stopped, aufhören). One lad after another lays down his tools to hear the song.

sentences.t: „mein Vater der mich aß,“
sentences.l: “my father, he who ate me,”

sentences.t: do höörden noch twe up un höörden dat,
sentences.l: then two more stopped and heard it,
sentences.n: “höörden … up” = hörten auf; “noch twe” = noch zwei (two more).

sentences.t: „mein Schwester der Marlenichen“
sentences.l: “my sister, little Marlene,”

sentences.t: do höörden wedder veer up,
sentences.l: then four more stopped,
sentences.n: “wedder veer” = wieder vier (again four).

sentences.t:
„sucht alle meine Benichen,
bindet sie in ein seiden Tuch,“
sentences.l:
“seeks out all my little bones,
binds them in a silken cloth,”

sentences.t: nu hackden noch man acht,
sentences.l: now only eight more were still hacking,
sentences.n: “nu” = nun; “noch man” = nur noch (only … still); “acht” = acht.

sentences.t: „legts unter“
sentences.l: “lays them under”

sentences.t: nu noch man fyw,
sentences.l: now only five more,
sentences.n: “fyw” = fünf (five).

sentences.t: „den Machandelbaum.“
sentences.l: “the juniper tree.”

sentences.t: nu noch man een.
sentences.l: now only one more.
sentences.n: “een” = einer (one). One lad still works as the song nears its end.

sentences.t: „Kywitt, kywitt, wat vör’n schöön Vagel bün ik!“
sentences.l: “Peewit, peewit, what a handsome bird am I!”

sentences.t: Do hüll de lezte ook up un hadd dat lezte noch höörd.
sentences.l: Then the last one stopped too and had heard the last part.
sentences.n: “hüll … up” = hielt/hörte auf; “lezte” = letzte.

sentences.t: „Vagel,“ secht he, „wat singst du schöön! laat my dat ook hören, sing my dat nochmaal.“
sentences.l: “Bird,” says he, “how beautifully you sing! let me hear it too, sing me that again.”
sentences.n: “singst” = singst; “laat” = lass (let!); “ook” = auch (too).

sentences.t: „Ne,“ secht de Vagel, „twemaal sing ik nich umsünst, gif my den Mählensteen, so will ik dat nochmaal singen.“
sentences.l: “No,” says the bird, “twice I do not sing for nothing, give me the millstone, then I will sing it again.”
sentences.n: “Mählensteen” = Mühlenstein (millstone) — the last, deadly gift, which the bird will drop on the stepmother.

sentences.t: „Ja,“ secht he, „wenn he my alleen tohöörd, so schullst du em hebben.“
sentences.l: “Yes,” says he, “if it belonged to me alone, you should have it.”
sentences.n: “alleen” = allein; “tohöörd” = zugehörte (belonged); “schullst” = solltest; “em” = ihn (it, the stone).

sentences.t: „Ja,“ säden de annern, „wenn he nochmaal singt, so schall he em hebben.“
sentences.l: “Yes,” said the others, “if it sings again, it shall have it.”
sentences.n: “säden” = sagten; “annern” = anderen; “schall” = soll.

sentences.t: Do köhm de Vagel herünn, un de Möllers saat’n all twintig mit Böhm an un böhrden den Steen up, „hu uh uhp, hu uh uhp, hu uh uhp!“
sentences.l: Then the bird came down, and the millers all twenty set to with levers and heaved the stone up, ‘hu uh up, hu uh up, hu uh up!’
sentences.i: Then the bird came down, and all twenty millers set to with poles and heaved the stone up, ‘heave-ho, heave-ho, heave-ho!’
sentences.n: “herünn” = herunter; “Möllers” = Müller; “saat’n … an” = saßen/setzten an (set to); “Böhm” = Bäume, here Hebebäume (levers); “böhrden … up” = hoben empor; “hu uh uhp” is their heaving-chant.

sentences.t: Do stöök de Vagel den Hals döör dat Lock un nöhm em üm as enen Kragen, un flöög wedder up den Boom un süng
sentences.l: Then the bird thrust its neck through the hole and took it round like a collar, and flew back up onto the tree and sang
sentences.n: “stöök” = stak/steckte (thrust); “döör dat Lock” = durch das Loch; “nöhm … üm” = nahm um; “Kragen” = Kragen (collar). The bird wears the millstone as its weapon.

sentences.t:
„mein Mutter der mich schlacht,
mein Vater der mich aß,
mein Schwester der Marlenichen
sucht alle meine Benichen,
bindt sie in ein seiden Tuch,
legts unter den Machandelbaum.
Kywitt, kywitt, wat vör’n schöön Vagel bün ik!“
sentences.l:
“my mother, she who slaughters me,
my father, he who ate me,
my sister, little Marlene,
seeks out all my little bones,
binds them in a silken cloth,
lays them under the juniper tree.
Peewit, peewit, what a handsome bird am I!”
sentences.n: The full song once more, now with the chain, the shoes and the millstone all gathered; the bird flies home to work its revenge.

sentences.t: Un as he dat uutsungen hadd, do deed he de Flünk von eenanner, un hadd in de rechte Klau de Kede un in de linke de Schö un üm den Hals den Mählensteen, un floog wyt wech na synes Vaders Huse.
sentences.l: And when he had sung that out, he spread his wings apart, and had in the right claw the chain and in the left the shoes and around his neck the millstone, and flew far away to his father’s house.
sentences.n: “deed … von eenanner” = tat auseinander (spread apart); “Flünk” = Flügel (wings); “floog” = flog; “synes Vaders Huse” = seines Vaters Hause.

sentences.t: In de Stuw seet de Vader, de Moder un Marleenken by Disch, un de Vader säd „ach, wat waart my licht, my is recht so good to Mode.“
sentences.l: In the room sat the father, the mother and Marleenken at the table, and the father said, “oh, how light I feel, I feel so truly good in spirit.”
sentences.n: “Stuw” = Stube (parlor); “waart my licht” = wird mir leicht (I feel glad); “good to Mode” = gut zumute (in good spirits). Father and stepmother now feel the opposite as the bird nears — he glad, she in dread.

sentences.t: „Nä,“ säd de Moder, „my is recht so angst, so recht as wenn en swoor Gewitter kummt.“
sentences.l: “Nay,” said the mother, “I am so full of dread, just as when a heavy thunderstorm is coming.”
sentences.n: “Nä” = nein; “my is … angst” = mir ist angst (I feel afraid — dative); “swoor Gewitter” = schweres Gewitter.

sentences.t: Marleenken awerst seet un weend un weend, da köhm de Vagel anflegen, un as he sik up dat Dack sett’t, „ach,“ säd de Vader, „my is so recht freudig un de Sünn schynt buten so schöön, my is recht, as schull ik enen olen Bekannten weddersehn.“
sentences.l: But Marleenken sat and wept and wept; then the bird came flying, and as it settled on the roof, “oh,” said the father, “I feel so truly glad, and the sun shines outside so beautifully; I feel just as if I should see an old acquaintance again.”
sentences.n: “anflegen” = angeflogen (come flying); “freudig” = freudig; “schynt buten” = scheint draußen; “olen Bekannten weddersehn” = alten Bekannten wiedersehen. The father half-senses his son’s return.

sentences.t: „Ne,“ säd de Fru, „my is so angst, de Täne klappern my, un dat is my as Führ in den Adern.“
sentences.l: “No,” said the wife, “I am so afraid, my teeth chatter, and it is to me like fire in the veins.”
sentences.n: “Täne klappern my” = die Zähne klappern mir; “Führ in den Adern” = Feuer in den Adern. Her guilt turns to terror.

sentences.t: Un se reet sik ehr Lyfken up un so mehr, awer Marleenken seet in en Eck un weend, un hadd eren Platen vör de Ogen, un weend den Platen ganß meßnatt.
sentences.l: And she tore her bodice open and more so, but Marleenken sat in a corner and wept, and held her apron before her eyes, and wept the apron quite soaking wet.
sentences.n: “reet … up” = riss auf; “Lyfken” = Leibchen (bodice); “Eck” = Ecke; “Platen” = Platen/Schürze (apron, pinafore); “meßnatt” = klatschnass (soaking wet).

sentences.t: Do sett’t sik de Vagel up den Machandelboom un süng
sentences.l: Then the bird settled on the juniper tree and sang
sentences.n: The bird returns to the juniper — where the mother lies and the bones were laid — to sing the song that will undo the stepmother.

sentences.t: „mein Mutter der mich schlacht,“
sentences.l: “my mother, she who slaughters me,”
sentences.n: The final singing, again broken up — each line answered by a burst of feeling in the house.

sentences.t: Do hüll de Moder de Oren to un kneep de Ogen to, un wull nich sehn un hören, awer dat bruusde ehr in de Oren as de allerstaarkste Storm, un de Ogen brennden ehr un zackden as Blitz.
sentences.l: Then the mother held her ears shut and pinched her eyes shut, and would not see or hear, but it roared in her ears like the very strongest storm, and her eyes burned and flashed like lightning.
sentences.n: “hüll … to” = hielt zu; “kneep … to” = kniff zu (pinched shut); “bruusde” = brauste (roared); “allerstaarkste” = allerstärkste; “brennden” = brannten; “zackden as Blitz” = zackten/zuckten wie Blitz.

sentences.t: „mein Vater der mich aß,“
sentences.l: “my father, he who ate me,”

sentences.t: „Ach, Moder,“ secht de Mann, „door is en schöön Vagel, de singt so herrlich, de Sünn schynt so warm, un dat rückt als luter Zinnemamen.“
sentences.l: “Oh, mother,” says the man, “there is a beautiful bird, it sings so gloriously, the sun shines so warm, and it smells like pure cinnamon.”
sentences.n: “rückt” = riecht (smells); “als luter Zinnemamen” = wie lauter Zimt (like pure cinnamon). The father, in bliss, still does not know his son sings above him.

sentences.t: „mein Schwester der Marlenichen“
sentences.l: “my sister, little Marlene,”

sentences.t: Do läd Marleenken den Kopp up de Knee un weend in eens wech, de Mann awerst säd „ik ga henuut, ik mutt den Vagel dicht by sehn.“
sentences.l: Then Marleenken laid her head on her knees and wept without ceasing, but the man said, “I am going out, I must see the bird close by.”
sentences.n: “läd” = legte; “in eens wech” = in einem weg (without ceasing); “ga henuut” = gehe hinaus; “mutt” = muss; “dicht by” = dicht bei (close by).

sentences.t: „Ach, gah nich,“ säd de Fru, „my is as beewd dat ganße Huus un stünn in Flammen.“
sentences.l: “Oh, don’t go,” said the wife, “I feel as though the whole house trembled and stood in flames.”
sentences.n: “gah nich” = geh nicht; “as beewd” = als bebte (as if it trembled); “Flammen” = Flammen.

sentences.t: Awerst de Mann güng henuut un seeg den Vagel an.
sentences.l: But the man went out and looked at the bird.

sentences.t:
„sucht alle meine Benichen,
bindt sie in ein seiden Tuch,
legts unter den Machandelbaum.
Kywitt, kywitt, wat vör’n schöön Vagel bün ik!“
sentences.l:
“seeks out all my little bones,
binds them in a silken cloth,
lays them under the juniper tree.
Peewit, peewit, what a handsome bird am I!”

sentences.t: Mit des leet de Vagel de gollne Kede fallen, un se feel dem Mann jüst um’n Hals, so recht hier herüm, dat se recht so schöön passd.
sentences.l: With that the bird let the golden chain fall, and it fell just around the man’s neck, right around here, so that it fitted just beautifully.
sentences.n: “leet … fallen” = ließ fallen; “feel” = fiel; “jüst um’n Hals” = just um den Hals; “passd” = passte (fitted). The father receives the chain — the bird rewards the one who was deceived.

sentences.t: Do güng he herin un säd „süh, wat is dat vör’n schöön Vagel, heft my so ’ne schöne gollne Kede schenkd, un süht so schöön uut.“
sentences.l: Then he went in and said, “look, what a beautiful bird that is; it has given me such a fine golden chain, and looks so beautiful.”
sentences.n: “herin” = herein; “süh” = sieh (look!); “heft … schenkd” = hat geschenkt (has given as a gift); “süht … uut” = sieht aus.

sentences.t: De Fru awerst wöör so angst, un füll langs in de Stuw hen, un de Mütz füll ehr von dem Kopp.
sentences.l: But the wife was so afraid, and fell full-length in the room, and the cap fell from her head.
sentences.n: “füll langs … hen” = fiel längs hin (fell full-length); “Mütz” = Mütze (cap); “füll” = fiel. Her dread mounts toward its end.

sentences.t: Do süng de Vagel wedder
sentences.l: Then the bird sang again

sentences.t: „mein Mutter der mich schlacht,“
sentences.l: “my mother, she who slaughters me,”

sentences.t: „Ach, dat ik dusend Föder ünner de Eerd wöör, dat ik dat nich hören schull!“
sentences.l: “Oh, that I were a thousand feet under the earth, that I might not have to hear it!”
sentences.i: “Oh, that I were a thousand feet beneath the earth, so that I need not hear it!”
sentences.n: “dusend Föder” = tausend Fuß (a thousand feet deep); “ünner de Eerd” = unter der Erde; “schull” = sollte. The stepmother’s despair.

sentences.t: „mein Vater der mich aß,“
sentences.l: “my father, he who ate me,”

sentences.t: Do füll de Fru vör dood nedder.
sentences.l: Then the wife fell down as if dead.
sentences.n: “füll … nedder” = fiel nieder (fell down); “vör dood” = wie tot (as if dead).

sentences.t: „mein Schwester der Marlenichen“
sentences.l: “my sister, little Marlene,”

sentences.t: „Ach,“ säd Marleenken, „ik will ook henuut gahn un sehn of de Vagel my wat schenkt?“ Do güng se henuut.
sentences.l: “Oh,” said Marleenken, “I too will go out and see whether the bird gives me something?” Then she went out.
sentences.n: “ook henuut gahn” = auch hinaus gehen; “of” = ob (whether); “schenkt” = schenkt. The bird’s three gifts now find their one each: chain to father, shoes to sister, stone to stepmother.

sentences.t: „sucht alle meine Benichen,
sentences.l: “seeks out all my little bones,

sentences.t:
bindt sie in ein seiden Tuch,“
Do smeet he ehr de Schöh herünn.
„legts unter den Machandelbaum.
Kywitt, kywitt, wat vör’n schöön Vagel bün ik!“
sentences.l:
binds them in a silken cloth,”
Then he threw the shoes down to her.
“lays them under the juniper tree.
Peewit, peewit, what a handsome bird am I!”
sentences.n: The narrator’s line drops in mid-song: “Do smeet he ehr de Schöh herünn” = da schmiss er ihr die Schuhe herunter (‘then he threw the shoes down to her’) — the bird gives Marleenken the red shoes just as she comes out. “Schöh” = Schuhe.

sentences.t: Do wöör ehr so licht un frölich.
sentences.l: Then she felt so light and merry.
sentences.n: “frölich” = fröhlich (merry).

sentences.t: Do truck se de neen roden Schö an, un danßd un sprüng herin.
sentences.l: Then she pulled the new red shoes on, and danced and sprang in.
sentences.n: “truck … an” = zog an (pulled on); “neen” = neuen (new); “danßd” = tanzte; “sprüng” = sprang; “herin” = herein.

sentences.t: „Ach,“ säd se, „ick wöör so trurig, as ik henuut güng, un nu is my so licht, dat is maal en herrlichen Vagel, hett my en Poor rode Schö schenkd.“
sentences.l: “Oh,” said she, “I was so sad when I went out, and now I feel so light; that is a glorious bird indeed, it has given me a pair of red shoes.”
sentences.n: “ick” = ich; “trurig” = traurig; “maal” = mal (indeed); “herrlichen” = herrlichen.

sentences.t: „Ne,“ säd de Fru un sprüng up, un de Hoor stünnen ehr to Baarg as Führsflammen, „my is as schull de Welt ünnergahn, ik will ook henuut, of my lichter warden schull.“
sentences.l: “No,” said the wife and sprang up, and her hair stood on end like flames of fire, “I feel as though the world must end; I too will go out, to see if I might feel lighter.”
sentences.i: “No,” said the wife, and sprang up, and her hair stood on end like tongues of flame, “I feel as though the world were coming to an end; I too will go out, to see whether it might ease me.”
sentences.n: “Hoor stünnen … to Baarg” = die Haare standen zu Berge (stood on end); “Führsflammen” = Feuersflammen; “ünnergahn” = untergehen (to perish); “of” = ob; “lichter warden schull” = leichter werden sollte. She goes to her death.

sentences.t: Un as se uut de Döhr köhm, bratsch! smeet ehr de Vagel den Mählensteen up den Kopp, dat se ganß tomatscht wurr.
sentences.l: And as she came out of the door — crash! — the bird threw the millstone onto her head, so that she was quite crushed to pulp.
sentences.i: And as she stepped out of the door — crash! — the bird flung the millstone down on her head, so that she was crushed to pulp.
sentences.n: The revenge, told as bluntly as the murder was: “smeet” = schmiss; “Mählensteen” = Mühlenstein; “tomatscht” = zermatscht (crushed to a pulp). The stepmother dies by the stone the bird earned with its song.

sentences.t: De Vader un Marleenken höörden dat un güngen henuut: do güng en Damp un Flamm un Führ up von der Städ, un as dat vorby wöör, do stünn de lütje Broder door, un he nöhm synen Vader un Marleenken by der Hand, un wören all dre so recht vergnöögt un güngen in dat Huus by Disch, un eeten.
sentences.l: The father and Marleenken heard it and went out: then a vapor and flame and fire rose up from the spot, and when it was over, there stood the little brother, and he took his father and Marleenken by the hand, and all three were so truly glad and went into the house to the table, and ate.
sentences.i: The father and Marleenken heard it and went out; then smoke and flame and fire rose from the spot, and when it was over, there stood the little brother, and he took his father and Marleenken by the hand, and all three were truly glad, and went into the house to the table, and ate.
sentences.n: “Damp” = Dampf (vapor); “Flamm” = Flamme; “up von der Städ” = auf von der Stätte (up from the spot); “lütje” = kleine; “nöhm” = nahm; “all dre” = alle drei; “vergnöögt” = vergnügt; “eeten” = aßen. The boy is reborn whole from the fire, and the family sits down together — the wish of the opening fulfilled.
